headline: Bobby Witt Jr. Goes First Overall, Shohei Drops to Two, and Someone Named Buttville Is Absolutely Going to Win This Thing
subheadline: The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft is in the books, and we've got steals, disasters, a reigning champion loading up, and one team whose name alone should disqualify them from holding a trophy.
body:
# The Definitive 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Goodfellas* where Henry Hill is narrating over a perfectly choreographed montage, explaining exactly how the whole operation works while everyone around him is either brilliant or getting whacked? That's a fantasy baseball draft. Fourteen managers, each convinced they're Henry Hill, each with a color-coded spreadsheet and a ranking system they built at 2 a.m. in February, each absolutely certain that the other thirteen are the guys who are going to get whacked. Someone's about to win. Someone's about to spend six months posting "injury luck" takes in the group chat. And at least three people made decisions tonight that will haunt them until Christmas.

The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft went down on March 22nd, and it did not disappoint. We had the first-overall debate resolved in the most decisive way possible. We had a reigning champion — **Sugar Land Skeeters**, fresh off their 2025 title — picking at 11 and somehow still looking dangerous. We had a perennial doormat (**General Hospital**, back-to-back 13th place finishes) swinging for its life. And we had at least two picks in the middle rounds that made me spit out my drink. The biggest steal of the draft happened in Round 2. The most puzzling decision happened in Round 5. And a team called **Buttville Skidmarks** — I'm going to need everyone to take a breath here — might have assembled the most quietly terrifying roster in the league. Fantasy baseball is a beautiful, stupid sport and I love every second of it.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

Let's get the big one out of the way: **North Correa**, holding the first overall pick, selected **Bobby Witt Jr.** of the Kansas City Royals. This is the right call. Witt is a five-category monster — a guy who hit .332 with 33 home runs, 109 RBIs, and 31 stolen bases while playing premium shortstop defense. He's the perfect fantasy anchor: durable, multi-category, ascending. North Correa has finished 14th and 12th the last two seasons, so this is their "we're not tanking anymore" moment. Pick two went to **Athletic Park Dragons** with **Shohei Ohtani** (batter-only), which in a 12-category head-to-head league is still an elite pick even without pitching contributions. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** took **Mookie Betts** at three — the multi-position eligibility alone (2B, SS, OF) makes him a luxury asset — and **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** grabbed **Aaron Judge** at four, which is exactly what you do when Aaron Judge is sitting there at four. The back half of Round 1 was where things got interesting: **Elly De La Cruz** to **BaseballPro77** at five is defensible given the stolen base upside, **José Ramírez** at six to **Albuquerque Bombers** is a gift, and **Juan Soto** at seven to **Hiroshima Radiation** might be the best value in the first round given what Soto does to OBP categories. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at eight, **Kyle Tucker** at nine — both excellent — and then the back end filled out with **Lindor**, **Tatis Jr.**, **Chourio**, **Corbin Carroll**, and **Julio Rodríguez**. Solid round overall. No disasters yet. That comes later.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** | Draft Slot: 1 | Grade: **B+**

They took **Bobby Witt Jr.** first overall and absolutely should have. Then in Round 2 they grabbed **Pete Alonso** — fresh off signing with Baltimore — who hit 34 homers in 2025. The pitching haul is genuinely interesting: **Logan Gilbert** in Round 3, then a pair of high-upside injury gambles in **Sandy Alcantara** (Round 8) and **Spencer Strider** (Round 9). If Alcantara is healthy, that's a Round 3 value at pick 112. If Strider is back to 2023 form, that's potentially the steal of the draft. Two massive "ifs," but North Correa has been rebuilding for two years and swinging for the fences on pitching depth is the right move. **Alex Bregman** to the Cubs in Round 4 gives them a third base anchor. This roster went from a 14th-place dumpster fire to legitimately competitive in one draft. The question is whether Alcantara and Strider are healthy, which is the same question it always is.

**Athletic Park Dragons** | Draft Slot: 2 | Grade: **B-**

**Shohei Ohtani** (batter) at two is correct. **Matt Olson** in Round 2 gives them 1B power — Olson hit 40 homers in 2025 and is a RBI machine. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 3 adds OBP and power. Then the Dragons went full pitching mode: **Michael King**, **Shota Imanaga**, **Aaron Nola**, **Luis Castillo** — that's four starting pitchers in rounds four through seven. It's an aggressive pitching stack that will dominate ERA and WHIP categories if everyone stays healthy, but their hitting depth beyond Ohtani/Olson/Schwarber is concerning. **Ezequiel Tovar** in Round 10 from Colorado is a quality-of-contact nightmare in that park. They finished 14th last year (a complete disaster after a 6th-place 2024 finish), and this draft is their attempt to stop the bleeding. The pitching-heavy approach is intriguing. The back-end bats are worrying.

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Draft Slot: 3 | Grade: **B**

**Mookie Betts** at three, **Ketel Marte** in Round 2 — that's a genuinely excellent 1-2 punch with premium position flexibility. **William Contreras** in Round 3 is a rock-solid catcher. The Reapers did something smart: they didn't panic on pitching until late, loading up on quality bats through Round 8 (including **Bryan Reynolds**, **Christian Yelich**, **Isaac Paredes**, and **Rhys Hoskins**). The pitching is thin — **Merrill Kelly** in Round 12 is their ace, which is a sentence that should concern everyone. Heavy on saves upside in the bullpen. They finished 10th in 2025 in their debut, and this draft suggests improvement, but without a true SP ace behind Mookie, the pitching categories could get ugly fast.

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Draft Slot: 4 | Grade: **A-**

The reigning 2024 champion (2023: 8th, 2024: 1st, 2025: 6th) continues to draft like the class of the league. **Aaron Judge** at four is obvious, but **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 is sneaky brilliant — Chisholm is a steals-and-power guy who qualifies at 2B, 3B, and OF, giving you lineup flexibility that wins head-to-head matchups. **Austin Riley** in Round 3 for third base depth. Then **Pablo López** in Round 4 anchors what becomes a quietly deep rotation. **Spencer Schwellenbach** and **Zac Gallen** in rounds six and seven add QS upside. **Brice Turang** in Round 8 gives them stolen base depth. This roster is balanced across all 12 categories, which is exactly what championship teams look like. The Twins drafted like a team that's been here before — because they have.

**BaseballPro77** | Draft Slot: 5 | Grade: **B+**

A 2023 champion (2023: 1st, 2024: 2nd, 2025: 7th) that knows how to win but slipped last year. **Elly De La Cruz** at five is the stolen base anchor this team needs. **Jackson Merrill** in Round 2 was excellent value — Merrill broke out in 2025 and his ADP had him going in the 20s. **Cole Ragans** in Round 3 for pitching. **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 4 locks up the NSVH category. The middle rounds feature **Junior Caminero** and **Hunter Greene** as high-ceiling fliers. **Luis Arraez** in Round 10 is the H/AB and OBP stabilizer every smart team needs — the man hits .330 in his sleep. Two championships in four years doesn't happen by accident. This roster has the balance to compete.

**Albuquerque Bombers** | Draft Slot: 6 | Grade: **A**

This might be my favorite top-half roster. **José Ramírez** at six is already value, but then the Bombers went out and grabbed **Freddie Freeman** in Round 2 (still elite, still a .300/30/100 guy), **Garrett Crochet** in Round 3 (incoming Boston Red Sox ace, led AL in strikeouts in 2025), and **Teoscar Hernández** in Round 4. **Logan Webb** in Round 5 adds a QS machine. **Willy Adames** in Round 6 gives them another power-speed middle infielder. The Bombers finished 3rd in 2025 and are clearly building toward a title — this is a 2026 championship roster if Crochet stays healthy in Boston. Deep bats, strong rotation, and a coherent strategy. This is how you draft.

**Hiroshima Radiation** | Draft Slot: 7 | Grade: **B-**

**Juan Soto** at seven is great value for OBP-heavy leagues. **Trea Turner** in Round 2 is excellent. But then they took **Blake Snell** in Round 3 — a guy who went 5-9 with a 3.94 ERA in 2025 and missed stretches of time — which feels optimistic. **Tyler Glasnow** in Round 5 is another injury-prone arm. The Radiation (2023: 2nd, 2024: 11th, 2025: 5th) are inconsistent because they keep gambling on arms that don't stay healthy. **Jose Altuve** in Round 4 is a nice bounce-back bet. **Anthony Santander** in Round 6 and **Jhoan Duran** in Round 11 (he was closing for Philly in 2025) add saves upside. The ceiling is high but the floor is the basement, which this franchise knows well from their 2024 crater.

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Draft Slot: 8 | Grade: **A-**

Back-to-back top-five finishes (2023: 4th, 2024: 5th, 2025: 2nd) and the Wonderdogs drafted like a team that smells blood. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at eight — fine — then **Bryce Harper** in Round 2? That's still elite production. **Corbin Burnes** in Round 3 is the pitching anchor this team needed. **Framber Valdez** in Round 4 for more rotation depth. And then — *and then* — **Ronald Acuña Jr.** in Round 5 at pick 64. After missing most of 2024 and fighting back in 2025, if Acuña is healthy in 2026, that's a top-10 overall player falling to pick 64. That's the sleeper pick of the draft. The Wonderdogs came to play.

**General Hospital** | Draft Slot: 10 | Grade: **C+**

Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. They need a miracle. **Francisco Lindor** at ten is solid. **Jarren Duran** in Round 2 gives them a legit speed-and-average threat. **Chris Sale** in Round 3... look, Chris Sale in 2025 had a resurgent year in Atlanta, but he's one windup away from a Tommy John. **Mike Trout** in Round 6 is either genius or tragic depending on whether Trout's knee holds up. There's a "hope and prayer" theme to this roster that tracks for a team finishing 13th twice. **Bryan Woo** in Round 8 is a smart buy-low. If every injury gamble hits, they're competing. They never all hit. *They never all hit.*

**Sugar Land Skeeters** | Draft Slot: 11 | Grade: **B+**

The reigning 2025 champions (2023: 13th to 2024: 3rd to 2025: 1st — one of the great redemption arcs in league history) pick 11th and still look dangerous. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** is back in OF-only eligibility with San Diego but is a 30/30 threat when healthy. **Tarik Skubal** in Round 2 is the steal of the draft's first two rounds — Skubal won the 2025 AL Cy Young and went in the mid-teens at pick 18? Robbery. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 4 adds another ace. **Ryan Helsley** in Round 7 locks saves. The Skeeters drafted like champions. They know how this works.

**Riverside Roughnecks** | Draft Slot: 12 | Grade: **B**

Three straight 9th-through-11th finishes. Solid but can't close. **Jackson Chourio** at 12 is the right pick — he's a 20/20 threat breaking out in Milwaukee. **Gunnar Henderson** in Round 2 is excellent value and gives them another premium SS bat. **Mason Miller** in Round 6 is a saves machine. **Roki Sasaki** in Round 7 at pick 96 is the most interesting gamble of the middle rounds — if the Dodgers have unlocked something with Sasaki, that's a top-30 SP at pick 96. Smart, balanced roster. The Roughnecks might finally have the depth to break through.

**Canberra Wombats** | Draft Slot: 13 | Grade: **B-**

Wildly inconsistent (2023: 3rd, 2024: 12th, 2025: 4th). **Corbin Carroll** at 13 — fine, though his 2025 numbers were a modest step back from his rookie explosion. **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 2 at pick 16 is outstanding value. **Josh Hader** in Round 5 locks saves. **Cal Raleigh** in Round 6 gives them catcher and power depth. **Félix Bautista** and **Shane McClanahan** in rounds nine and thirteen add upside if healthy. The Wombats draft well but execute inconsistently — this roster has a similar feel to their 2023 and 2025 drafts, which both produced top-four finishes. Maybe the third time is the charm.

**Benchwarmers** | Draft Slot: 14 | Grade: **A-**

The snake's tail delivered a gift. **Julio Rodríguez** at 14 and then **Paul Skenes** at 15 (Round 2, pick 15) — that's a back-to-back 1-2 punch that no other team in this draft can match for value at that position in the snake. Skenes was the most electric young arm in baseball in 2025 and dropped to pick 15 because of draft position optics. Brilliant. **Oneil Cruz** in Round 3 for upside. **Dylan Cease** in Round 4 deepens the rotation. **Edwin Díaz** in Round 5 locks saves. **Adley Rutschman** in Round 6 is the best catcher in the game. The Benchwarmers (2023: 14th, 2024: 7th, 2025: 9th) are trending up, and this draft might be the one that gets them over the top.

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**Three Best Value Picks:**

1. **Tarik Skubal**, Round 2, Pick 18 (**Sugar Land Skeeters**) — The reigning AL Cy Young winner going 18th overall in a 14-team draft is either a glitch in the matrix or everyone collectively forgot what year it is. Skubal was the best pitcher in the American League last season. This is a Round 1 arm going in Round 2. Steal of the draft.

2. **Paul Skenes**, Round 2, Pick 15 (**Benchwarmers**) — Similar energy. The most hyped young pitcher in the sport, taken with the 15th pick of the whole draft because the Benchwarmers held the last pick in Round 1. Snake drafts giveth, and this one gave *hard*.

3. **Ronald Acuña Jr.**, Round 5, Pick 64 (**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**) — If healthy, this is a top-10 overall player. He went 64th. If that's not the most voltage pick of the draft, I don't know what is. High risk, potentially insane reward.

**Three Worst Reaches or Head-Scratchers:**

1. **Blake Snell**, Round 3, Pick 35 (**Hiroshima Radiation**) — Snell is talented and maddening in equal measure. His 2025 was bumpy, his health history is a cautionary tale, and there were better pitching options available here. Classic Hiroshima: big name, big risk, big potential heartbreak.

2. **Mike Trout**, Round 6, Pick 75 (**General Hospital**) — I love Mike Trout the baseball player. I have complicated feelings about Mike Trout the fantasy asset in 2026, given the knee situation. General Hospital has now finished 13th twice and is gambling its season on a player whose last healthy 150-game campaign feels like ancient history. Bold. Possibly reckless.

3. **Ezequiel Tovar**, Round 10, Pick 139 (**Athletic Park Dragons**) — Tovar has talent, but drafting a Coors Field bat in a categories league where batting average matters is a trap. His road numbers make you want to look away. Puzzling in Round 10 when better options were on the board.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **Buttville Skidmarks win the title.** I know. I KNOW. But **Kyle Tucker** plus **Zack Wheeler** plus **Rafael Devers** plus **Corey Seager** plus **Jacob deGrom** (if healthy) is a complete roster. They've hovered around 8th for three years and this is the year the talent finally cashes out.

2. **General Hospital finishes last for the third straight year.** The **Mike Trout** gamble doesn't pay off, **Chris Sale** gets hurt by June, and the team that has finished 13th twice proves it wasn't bad luck — it was a philosophy.

3. **Tarik Skubal leads the league in pitching category wins** for **Sugar Land Skeeters** and they become the first team in league history to repeat as champions.

4. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** makes the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** the most talked-about team in the league by August — either because he's the best fantasy player alive again, or because he got hurt and everyone is writing the eulogy. There is no middle ground with Acuña.

5. **Albuquerque Bombers** win their first ever championship. **Garrett Crochet** goes 18-6 with 220 strikeouts in Boston, **José Ramírez** posts his usual .280/30/100/25SB line, and the Bombers — who've never finished higher than 3rd — finally break through. Sometimes the most balanced roster in the room just *wins*.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Here's my title pick: **Albuquerque Bombers**.

The case is airtight. They have **José Ramírez** — one of the three best fantasy players in baseball, full stop — anchoring a lineup that also includes **Freddie Freeman**, **Teoscar Hernández**, **Willy Adames**, and **Colton Cowser**. That's runs, RBIs, average, and stolen bases covered from multiple angles. The rotation features **Garrett Crochet** (who led the AL in strikeouts last season before moving to Boston), **Logan Webb** (a QS machine who eats innings like it's his job, because it is), **Hunter Brown**, and **Justin Steele**. That's a rotation that will compete in ERA, WHIP, strikeouts, and quality starts every single week.

They finished 3rd in 2025. They finished 10th in 2024. They finished 5th in 2023. This is a franchise that has been knocking on the door for three years, drafting well enough to compete but never quite assembling the pieces to close. This draft is different. This draft has no obvious weaknesses. This draft has the ace (Crochet), the anchor (Ramírez), the depth (Freeman/Webb/Hernández), and the saves coverage (Pete Fairbanks in Round 12).

Fantasy baseball, like most things in life, rewards patience and punishes panic. The **Albuquerque Bombers** have been patient. They have not panicked. And on March 22nd, 2026, in a MillerLite®-sponsored draft that will be talked about in this league for years, they made the picks that champions make.

Raise the banner. Albuquerque's time is now.

*(And if I'm wrong, it was Buttville all along — and somehow that's an equally satisfying ending.)*
